gui.tutorials.gettingStarted.addMoveBloc: Drag the move 10 steps block from the Motion category into the workspace.
gui.tutorials.gettingStarted.addSayBlock: Now drag the say hello for 2 seconds block from the Looks category and snap it below the move block.
gui.tutorials.gettingStarted.addGreenFla: Finally, drag the when green flag clicked block from Events and place it on top of your blocks. Then click the green flag to run your project!
gui.tutorials.addSprite.step1: To add a new sprite, click the Choose a Sprite button at the bottom right of the screen. Pick any sprite you like!
gui.tutorials.addBackdrop.step1: Click the Choose a Backdrop button at the bottom right of the screen to add a new backdrop to your project.
gui.tutorials.switchCostume.step1: Use the switch costume block inside a forever loop to animate your sprite. Each costume is like a frame of animation!
gui.tutorials.moveArrowKeys.stepLeftRigh: Use when key pressed blocks for the left and right arrow keys. Add change x blocks to move the sprite left and right.
gui.tutorials.moveArrowKeys.stepUpDown: Now add when key pressed blocks for the up and down arrow keys. Use change y blocks to move the sprite up and down.
gui.tutorials.changeSize.step1: Use the change size block to make your sprite grow or shrink. Put it in a forever loop to keep changing!
gui.tutorials.glideAround.stepBackAndFor: Use the glide block to make your sprite move smoothly back and forth across the stage.
gui.tutorials.glideAround.stepPoint: Use the glide to x y block to make your sprite glide to a specific point on the stage.
gui.tutorials.spin.stepTurn: Use the turn block inside a forever loop to make your sprite spin continuously.
gui.tutorials.spin.stepPointInDirection: Use the point in direction block to set which way your sprite faces before spinning.
gui.tutorials.recordASound.stepSoundsTab: Click on the Sounds tab at the top of the screen to open the sound editor.
gui.tutorials.recordASound.stepClickReco: Click the Record button to start recording a new sound.
gui.tutorials.recordASound.stepPressReco: Press the record button and speak into your microphone to record your sound.
gui.tutorials.recordASound.stepChooseSou: After recording, you can trim and edit your sound. Press Save to keep it.
gui.tutorials.recordASound.stepPlayYourS: Now use the play sound block to play your recorded sound in your project!
gui.tutorials.hideAndShow.step1: Use the hide and show blocks to make your sprite disappear and reappear. Add a wait block in between for a timed effect!
gui.tutorials.animateAName.stepPickLette: Click the Choose a Sprite button and pick a letter sprite to start spelling out a name.
gui.tutorials.animateAName.stepPlaySound: Add a when this sprite clicked block and a play sound block so the letter plays a sound when you click it.
gui.tutorials.animateAName.stepPickLette: Choose another letter sprite to add the next letter of your name.
gui.tutorials.animateAName.stepChangeCol: Add a change color effect block to make this letter change color when clicked.
gui.tutorials.animateAName.stepSpin: Pick another letter and add a turn block inside a forever loop to make it spin.
gui.tutorials.animateAName.stepGrow: Pick one more letter and add a change size block to make it grow when clicked.
gui.tutorials.animateACharacter.stepPick: Click the Choose a Backdrop button and pick a backdrop for your scene.
Click the Choose a Sprite button and pick a character for your animation.
gui.tutorials.animateACharacter.stepSayS: Add a say block to make your character say something when the green flag is clicked.
gui.tutorials.animateACharacter.stepAddS: Add a play sound block to give your character a sound effect.
gui.tutorials.animateACharacter.stepTalk: Use the switch costume block to animate your character talking by switching between costumes.
gui.tutorials.animateACharacter.stepMove: Add when key pressed blocks with change x blocks so your character can move left and right with the arrow keys.
gui.tutorials.animateACharacter.stepJump: Use change y blocks to make your character jump up and come back down.
gui.tutorials.animateACharacter.stepChan: Add a change color effect block to make your character change colors.
gui.tutorials.tellAStory.stepPickBackdro: Click the Choose a Backdrop button and pick a backdrop for your story scene.
gui.tutorials.tellAStory.stepPickSprite: Click the Choose a Sprite button and pick a character for your story.
gui.tutorials.tellAStory.stepSaySomethin: Add a say block to make your character say something as part of the story.
gui.tutorials.tellAStory.stepPickSprite2: Add another character sprite so two characters can have a conversation.
gui.tutorials.tellAStory.stepFlip: Flip the direction of the second character so the two characters face each other.
gui.tutorials.tellAStory.stepConversatio: Use say and wait blocks on both characters with different timing to create a conversation.
gui.tutorials.tellAStory.stepPickBackdro: Add another backdrop for a new scene in your story.
gui.tutorials.tellAStory.stepSwitchBackd: Use the switch backdrop block to change scenes in your story.
gui.tutorials.tellAStory.stepHideCharact: Use the hide block to make a character disappear when the scene changes.
gui.tutorials.tellAStory.stepShowCharact: Use the show block to make a character appear in the new scene.
gui.tutorials.sayItOutLoud.stepAddExtens: Click the Add Extension button at the bottom left and choose the Text to Speech extension to add talking blocks.
gui.tutorials.sayItOutLoud.stepSaySometh: Drag the speak block into the workspace and type what you want your character to say out loud.
gui.tutorials.sayItOutLoud.stepSetVoice: Use the set voice block to choose a different voice for your character.
gui.tutorials.sayItOutLoud.stepMoveAroun: Add motion blocks to make your character move while it talks.
gui.tutorials.sayItOutLoud.stepAddBackdr: Click the Choose a Backdrop button and pick a backdrop for your talking animation.
gui.tutorials.sayItOutLoud.stepAddSprite: Add another sprite to create a conversation between two characters.
gui.tutorials.sayItOutLoud.stepSong: Use the speak block with different words and set voice blocks to make your character sing a song.
gui.tutorials.sayItOutLoud.stepChangeCol: Add a change color effect block to make your character change color while speaking.
gui.tutorials.sayItOutLoud.stepSpin: Add a turn block to make your character spin around while it talks.
gui.tutorials.sayItOutLoud.stepGrowShrin: Use the change size block to make your character grow and shrink while speaking.
gui.tutorials.imagine.stepTypeWhatYouWan: Click on the say block and type what you want your character to say.
gui.tutorials.imagine.stepClickGreenFlag: Click the green flag to see your character say the message you typed.
gui.tutorials.imagine.stepChooseBackdrop: Click the Choose a Backdrop button and pick any backdrop to set the scene for your world.
gui.tutorials.imagine.stepChooseSprite: Click the Choose a Sprite button and pick any sprite to add to your world.
gui.tutorials.imagine.stepFlyAround: Add a when space key pressed block with a glide block to make your sprite fly around when you press space.
gui.tutorials.imagine.stepChooseAnotherS: Add another sprite to your world to make it more interesting.
gui.tutorials.imagine.stepLeftRight: Use the change x block with when key pressed to move this sprite left and right.
gui.tutorials.imagine.stepUpDown: Use the change y block with when key pressed to move this sprite up and down.
gui.tutorials.imagine.stepChangeCostumes: Add a next costume block to make your sprite animate by switching between costumes.
gui.tutorials.imagine.stepGlideToPoint: Use the glide to x y block to make your sprite glide to a specific location.
gui.tutorials.imagine.stepGrowShrink: Use the change size block to make your sprite grow and shrink.
gui.tutorials.imagine.stepChooseAnotherB: Add another backdrop to create a new scene in your world.
gui.tutorials.imagine.stepSwitchBackdrop: Use the switch backdrop block to change between different scenes.
gui.tutorials.imagine.stepRecordASound: Click the Sounds tab and add or record a sound to play in your world.
gui.tutorials.imagine.stepChooseSound: Choose a sound from the library and add a play sound block to your code.
gui.tutorials.addEffects.step1: Use the change effect block to add visual effects like color, fisheye, whirl, pixelate, mosaic, brightness, and ghost to your sprite.
gui.tutorials.makeItFly.stepChooseBackdr: Click the Choose a Backdrop button and pick a sky background for your flying game.
gui.tutorials.makeItFly.stepChooseCharac: Choose a character sprite that will fly around the screen.
gui.tutorials.makeItFly.stepSaySomething: Add a say block to make your flying character say something when the game starts.
gui.tutorials.makeItFly.stepMoveArrows: Add when key pressed blocks with change x and change y blocks to control your character with arrow keys.
gui.tutorials.makeItFly.stepChooseObject: Add another sprite that your character will try to collect as it flies around.
gui.tutorials.makeItFly.stepFlyingObject: Add code to make the collectible object move across the screen and reappear.
gui.tutorials.makeItFly.stepSelectFlying: Click on your flying character sprite to select it and add more code.
gui.tutorials.makeItFly.stepAddScore: Create a variable called score to keep track of how many objects you collect.
gui.tutorials.makeItFly.stepKeepScore: Add a change score by 1 block inside an if touching block so the score goes up when you collect an object.
gui.tutorials.makeItFly.stepAddScenery: Add a scenery sprite like clouds or buildings to make the background more interesting.
gui.tutorials.makeItFly.stepMoveScenery: Add code to make the scenery move across the screen to create a scrolling effect.
gui.tutorials.makeItFly.stepSwitchLooks: Use the next costume block to change the scenery appearance as it moves.
gui.tutorials.makeMusic.stepPickInstrume: Click the Choose a Sprite button and pick an instrument sprite to use in your music project.
gui.tutorials.makeMusic.stepPlaySound: Add a when this sprite clicked block and a play sound block so the instrument plays when you click it.
gui.tutorials.makeMusic.stepMakeSong: Add multiple play sound blocks with different notes to create a song that plays automatically.
gui.tutorials.makeMusic.stepMakeBeat: Pick a drum sprite and add play drum blocks to create a beat.
gui.tutorials.makeMusic.stepMakeBeatbox: Pick the microphone sprite and add random play sound blocks for a surprise beatbox effect.
gui.tutorials.pong.stepAddBackdrop: Click the Choose a Backdrop button and pick a simple backdrop for your Pong game.
gui.tutorials.pong.stepAddBallSprite: Add a ball sprite that will bounce around the stage.
gui.tutorials.pong.stepBounceAround: Add code to make the ball move and bounce off the edges of the stage.
gui.tutorials.pong.stepAddPaddle: Add a paddle sprite at the bottom of the stage.
gui.tutorials.pong.stepMoveThePaddle: Add code to make the paddle follow your mouse pointer left and right.
gui.tutorials.pong.stepSelectBallSprite: Click on the ball sprite to select it and add more code.
gui.tutorials.pong.stepAddMoreCodeToBall: Add an if touching paddle block to make the ball bounce off the paddle.
gui.tutorials.pong.stepAddAScore: Create a variable called score to track how many times you hit the ball.
gui.tutorials.pong.stepChooseScoreFromMe: Select the score variable from the dropdown menu in the change block.
gui.tutorials.pong.stepInsertChangeScore: Add the change score by 1 block inside the if touching paddle section so the score goes up each time you hit the ball.
gui.tutorials.pong.stepResetScore: Add a set score to 0 block at the start so the score resets when you begin a new game.
gui.tutorials.pong.stepAddLineSprite: Add a line sprite at the bottom of the stage to mark the game over zone.
gui.tutorials.pong.stepGameOver: Add code to stop the game when the ball touches the line at the bottom.
gui.tutorials.makeAGame.stepPickSprite: Click the Choose a Sprite button and pick a sprite to be your clickable target.
gui.tutorials.makeAGame.stepPlaySound: Add a when this sprite clicked block and a play sound block so the sprite makes a sound when you click it.
gui.tutorials.makeAGame.stepAddScore: Create a variable called score to track how many times you click the sprite.
gui.tutorials.makeAGame.stepChangeScore: Add a change score by 1 block inside the when this sprite clicked section to increase the score each time.
gui.tutorials.makeAGame.stepRandomPositi: Add a go to random position block so the sprite moves to a new spot after each click.
gui.tutorials.makeAGame.stepChangeColor: Add a change color effect block to make the sprite change color each time it is clicked.
gui.tutorials.makeAGame.stepResetScore: Add a set score to 0 block under when green flag clicked so the score resets at the start.
gui.tutorials.chaseGame.stepAddBackdrop: Click the Choose a Backdrop button and pick an underwater or outdoor backdrop for your chase game.
gui.tutorials.chaseGame.stepAddSprite1: Add a sprite that you will control to chase after objects.
gui.tutorials.chaseGame.stepRightLeft: Add when key pressed blocks for right and left arrow keys with change x blocks to move the sprite.
gui.tutorials.chaseGame.stepUpDown: Add when key pressed blocks for up and down arrow keys with change y blocks to move the sprite.
gui.tutorials.chaseGame.stepAddSprite2: Add another sprite that will move randomly for your character to chase.
gui.tutorials.chaseGame.stepMoveRandomly: Add a glide to random position block inside a forever loop to make this sprite move randomly.
gui.tutorials.chaseGame.stepPlaySound: Select your character sprite and add an if touching block with a play sound block inside it.
gui.tutorials.chaseGame.stepAddVariable: Create a variable called score to track how many times you catch the other sprite.
gui.tutorials.chaseGame.stepChangeScore: Add a change score by 1 block inside the if touching section so the score goes up each time you catch the sprite.
gui.tutorials.codeCartoon.stepSaySomethi: Add a when green flag clicked block and a say block to make a character say something when the project starts.
gui.tutorials.codeCartoon.stepAnimate: Add a when this sprite clicked block with switch costume blocks to animate a character when you click on it.
gui.tutorials.codeCartoon.stepSelectDiff: Click on a different character sprite in the sprite list to select it.
gui.tutorials.codeCartoon.stepUseMinusSi: Use a minus number in the change size block to make a character shrink when clicked.
gui.tutorials.codeCartoon.stepGrowShrink: Add change size blocks with positive and negative numbers in a loop to make a character grow and shrink.
gui.tutorials.codeCartoon.stepSelectDiff: Click on another character sprite in the sprite list to add code to it.
gui.tutorials.codeCartoon.stepJump: Use change y blocks with positive and negative numbers to make the character jump up and come back down.
gui.tutorials.codeCartoon.stepChangeScen: Add a when this sprite clicked block with a switch backdrop block to change scenes when you click a character.
gui.tutorials.codeCartoon.stepGlideAroun: Add a glide block to make a character glide smoothly across the stage.
gui.tutorials.codeCartoon.stepChangeCost: Use the next costume block in a loop to animate a character by cycling through its costumes.
gui.tutorials.codeCartoon.stepChooseMore: Click the Choose a Sprite button to add more characters and keep building your cartoon!
gui.tutorials.cartoonNetwork.stepShowCha: Pick a character sprite and add a show block so it appears on screen.
gui.tutorials.cartoonNetwork.stepSay: Add a say block to make your character introduce themselves or give instructions.
gui.tutorials.cartoonNetwork.stepGlide: Add a glide block to make your character move smoothly across the stage.
gui.tutorials.cartoonNetwork.stepPickSpr: Add another sprite that your character will chase and collect.
gui.tutorials.cartoonNetwork.stepCollect: Add code to detect when your character touches the object and collect it.
gui.tutorials.cartoonNetwork.stepVariabl: Create a variable called score to keep track of how many objects you collect.
gui.tutorials.cartoonNetwork.stepScore: Add a change score by 1 block so the score increases each time you collect an object.
gui.tutorials.cartoonNetwork.stepBackdro: Add code to switch to a new backdrop when the score reaches a certain number to level up.
gui.tutorials.videoSensing.stepAddExtens: Click the Add Extension button at the bottom left and choose the Video Sensing extension.
gui.tutorials.videoSensing.stepPet: Add a when video motion is greater than block to detect when you wave your hand near the cat sprite.
gui.tutorials.videoSensing.stepAnimate: Add a next costume block so the sprite animates when it detects video motion.
gui.tutorials.videoSensing.stepPop: Add a balloon sprite and use video sensing to pop it when you wave your hand over it.
gui.tutorials.talking.stepAddExtension: Click the Add Extension button and choose the Text to Speech extension to add talking blocks.
gui.tutorials.talking.stepChooseSprite: Click the Choose a Sprite button and pick a character for your tale.
gui.tutorials.talking.stepSaySomething: Add a speak block and type what you want the character to say out loud.
gui.tutorials.talking.stepChooseBackdrop: Click the Choose a Backdrop button and pick a backdrop for your first scene.
gui.tutorials.talking.stepSwitchBackdrop: Add a when this sprite clicked block with a next backdrop block to change scenes when you click the character.
gui.tutorials.talking.stepChooseAnotherS: Add another character sprite to your tale.
gui.tutorials.talking.stepMoveAround: Add motion blocks to make this character move around the stage.
gui.tutorials.talking.stepChooseAnotherB: Add another backdrop for a new scene in your tale.
gui.tutorials.talking.stepAnimateTalking: Use switch costume blocks to animate the character's mouth moving while it speaks.
gui.tutorials.talking.stepChooseThirdBac: Add a third backdrop for the final scene of your tale.
gui.tutorials.talking.stepChooseSound: Go to the Sounds tab and add a music track from the sound library for your characters to dance to.
gui.tutorials.talking.stepDanceMoves: Add turn and move blocks to make your character dance when the music plays.
gui.tutorials.talking.stepAskAnswer: Use the ask block from the Sensing category to let the user interact with your tale by typing an answer.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.boardMenu: Click the Board menu in the top menu bar to see board options.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.boardManager: Click Board Manager from the dropdown. This opens the board selection dialog.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.searchUno: Type UNO in the search box to find the Arduino Uno board.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.selectUno: Click on the Arduino Uno card to select it.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.addBoard: Click the Add Board button to add the Arduino blocks to your toolbox.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.dismissConnection: A device connection dialog will appear. Close it by clicking the X button — we will use the simulator instead of a real board.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.uploadMode: Click the Upload toggle at the top to switch to Upload mode. This shows the Code and Simulator tabs on the right side.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.findSetup: Click the Arduino category in the toolbox and scroll down to the Code Generation section. You will find the setup and loop block at the bottom.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.dragSetup: Drag the setup/loop block into the workspace. This is the main block for Arduino programs. Setup runs once when the program starts, and loop runs over and over.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.addPinMode: Drag a pinMode block into the setup section. Set pin to 13 and mode to output. This tells the Arduino that pin 13 will be used to send signals out.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.dwHigh: Drag a digitalWrite block into the loop section. Set pin to 13 and value to high. This turns the LED on.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.delay1: Add a delay block after digitalWrite. Set it to 1000 milliseconds which is 1 second. The LED will stay on for this long.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.dwLow: Add another digitalWrite block. Keep pin 13 but change the value to low. This turns the LED off.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.delay2: Add one more delay block with 1000 milliseconds. Your blink program is now complete! The loop will repeat: LED on, wait, LED off, wait.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.simulator: Click the Simulator tab on the right side. The simulator lets you test your code without a real Arduino board.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.openPicker: Click the plus button at the bottom right to open the component picker.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.selectLed: Select LED from the component list to add it to the simulator.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.pinMapping: In the pin mapping dialog, select pin 13 from the dropdown and click Save. This connects the virtual LED to the same pin your code uses.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.ledAdded: You can now see the LED component on the simulator board, connected to pin 13.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.clickStart: Click the Start button at the top of the simulator. Your program will be compiled and sent to the virtual Arduino board.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.compiling: Wait a few seconds while the program compiles. You will see a loading indicator. Once it finishes, the simulation will start automatically.
gui.tutorials.blinkLed.running: The LED is now blinking on and off every second! Congratulations, you have created your first Arduino program! Try changing the delay values to make it blink faster or slower.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.intro: Every doorbell, elevator button, and keyboard key on your laptop does one tiny thing: it senses a press and switches something else on. Today you'll teach an LED that exact trick. Press the button — the light comes on. Let go — the light goes off. Before you start, make sure Arduino Uno is selected and you're in Upload mode. If you haven't done that yet, finish the Blink an LED tutorial first.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.setupLoop: Open the Arduino category and drag the setup-and-loop block into the workspace. Just like Scratch has 'when green flag clicked' and 'forever', Arduino has setup and loop. Setup runs once when the program starts. Loop runs forever, over and over, while the Arduino has power.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.pinModeLed: An Arduino has rows of labelled holes around the edge of the board — those are the pins. Each pin is a wire connection point. Some pins send voltage out (to lights, motors). Some pins listen for voltage coming in (from buttons, sensors). The Arduino needs to know which is which. Drag a pinMode block into setup. Set pin to 13 and mode to output — pin 13 will be sending voltage out to the LED.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.ifElse: We want the LED to react differently when the button is pressed vs not pressed. That's exactly what an if-else block is for — same block you've seen in Scratch. Open the Control category and drag an if-else block into the loop section. Whatever question we put in its diamond slot will run the if branch when the answer is yes, and the else branch when the answer is no.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.writeHigh: Drag a digitalWrite block into the if branch — the top half of the if-else. Set pin to 13 and value to high. When the if-question's answer is yes (button pressed), this line runs: 5 volts on pin 13, LED lights up.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.writeLow: Drag another digitalWrite into the else branch — the bottom half. Pin 13, value low. When the question's answer is no (button not pressed), this line runs instead: 0 volts on pin 13, LED dark. The blocks are done — now let's wire up the simulator.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.addLed: Open the Simulator tab. Click the plus button at the bottom-right and choose LED. In the pin dialog, choose pin 13 and click Save. The virtual LED appears next to the virtual Arduino — wired to the same pin your code is writing high and low to.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.addButton: Click the plus button again, choose Push Button, set pin to 2, click Save. The button is now wired between pin 2 and… well, that's exactly what the next step is about.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.start: Click the Start button at the top of the simulator. Your program loads into the virtual Arduino — give it a few seconds. The simulator goes live.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.running: Click and hold the simulated button. The LED lights up. Let go — it goes dark. You just built a real-world reactive program. The same trick is inside every doorbell, every light switch, every keyboard key, every elevator button you've ever pressed.
gui.tutorials.htmlHello.addExtension: Click the purple Add Extension button at the bottom left of the screen to open the Extension Library.
gui.tutorials.htmlHello.progFilter: Click the Programming tab at the top to filter the extensions. You will see the HTML 5 extension.
gui.tutorials.htmlHello.selectHtml5: Click the HTML 5 card to add the HTML extension to your project. This gives you all the blocks needed to build web pages.
gui.tutorials.htmlHello.uploadMode: Click the Upload toggle at the top right to switch to Upload mode. This shows the HTML and View tabs on the right side.
gui.tutorials.htmlHello.category: Click the HTML5 category in the blocks panel on the left. You will see all the blocks needed to build a web page.
gui.tutorials.htmlHello.dragHtml: Find the html block at the top of the HTML5 flyout and drag it into the workspace. Every web page starts with this block.
gui.tutorials.htmlHello.addHead: Drag the head block and snap it below the html block. The head section holds information about your page like its title.
gui.tutorials.htmlHello.addTitle: Drag the title block inside the head block. Click the text field and type Hello Web. This text appears in the browser tab.
gui.tutorials.htmlHello.addBody: Drag the body block and snap it below the head block. Everything visible on your web page goes inside the body.
gui.tutorials.htmlHello.addH1: Drag the heading block inside the body block. Keep the level at 1 for a large heading. Click the text field and type Hello, Web!
gui.tutorials.htmlHello.addParagraph: Drag a paragraph block and snap it below the heading inside the body. Click the text field and type This is my first web page!
gui.tutorials.htmlHello.htmlTab: Click the HTML tab on the right panel. You can see the real HTML code that your blocks have generated!
gui.tutorials.htmlHello.viewTab: Click the View tab to see your web page rendered in the browser. Congratulations — you have built your first HTML page!
gui.tutorials.pythonHello.typeSay: Click inside the Python editor and type exactly this: say("Hello World!") — with the round brackets and the quotes. The word say is a CodeSkool helper — it only works here, not in regular Python on your laptop. When you run it, it will make your penguin speak with a speech bubble on the stage. You can change the words inside the quotes to anything you like — your name, a joke, anything — just keep the word say and the brackets.
gui.tutorials.pythonHello.runSay: Now click the Run button in the toolbar below the code editor. Watch the stage on the right — a speech bubble pops up right next to the penguin with your message! That's what say does: it puts your words on the stage as a visual speech bubble, attached to the sprite. Cool, right? But say is only one way Python can show us text. There's another way — and it looks completely different.
gui.tutorials.pythonHello.typePrint: Press Enter to go to line 2, then type print("Hello World!"). Now look closely — print is different from say. print is standard Python — it's built into Python itself and works in every Python program ever written, not just CodeSkool. But print doesn't put a speech bubble on the stage. It sends your message somewhere else. Want to know where?
gui.tutorials.ledGlowEffect.placeholder: You'll learn how to use PWM to fade an LED smoothly brighter and dimmer, like a heartbeat.
gui.tutorials.trafficLight.placeholder: You'll build a working traffic light with red, yellow and green LEDs — cycling through the lights just like a real one.
gui.tutorials.sosMorseCode.placeholder: You'll blink an LED with the SOS Morse signal — three short flashes, three long flashes, three short flashes — the universal call for help.
gui.tutorials.nightLamp.placeholder: You'll build a night lamp that turns an LED on automatically when the room gets dark and off when it's bright.
gui.tutorials.securityAlarm.placeholder: You'll build a security alarm that sounds a buzzer when a motion sensor spots someone sneaking past.
gui.tutorials.fanSpeedControl.placeholde: You'll control the speed of a fan by turning a knob — smoothly speeding it up or slowing it down, just like a real dimmer.
gui.tutorials.automaticPlantIrrigation.p: You'll build an automatic plant waterer that reads a soil moisture sensor and switches on a pump whenever the soil gets too dry.
gui.tutorials.carParkingSensor.placehold: You'll build a parking sensor with an ultrasonic distance module — the buzzer beeps faster as the car gets closer to the wall, just like a real car.
gui.tutorials.automaticHandSanitizer.pla: You'll build a touchless sanitizer dispenser — the ultrasonic sensor spots a hand and a servo squirts the sanitizer, all without anyone touching anything.
gui.tutorials.smartDoorLock.placeholder: You'll build a smart door lock that opens with a secret keypad code — type the right code and a servo unlocks the door, type a wrong one and it stays shut.
gui.tutorials.smartPeopleCounter.placeho: You'll build a people counter that ticks up by one every time someone walks past an infrared sensor — just like the counters at shop doors.
gui.tutorials.pythonHello.workspace: Before we code, let's look around. The white area on the left is the Python editor — that's where you type code. The penguin on the right lives on the stage — that's where anything visual happens when you run your code. And the dark black strip at the bottom is the terminal — a place where your program can print text messages. Three places, three jobs. Let's use them!
gui.tutorials.pythonHello.terminal: See that dark black strip at the bottom of the screen? That's the terminal — some people call it the console. It's a place where Python programs write text for you to read. The stage shows visual things like sprites and speech bubbles. The terminal shows words — plain text, one line after another. Right now the terminal is empty because we haven't run the new print line yet. Let's do it!
gui.tutorials.pythonHello.runBoth: Click Run one more time. Two things happen at the same time: the penguin says Hello World! on the stage (that's the say line), AND your message appears in the terminal at the bottom (that's the print line). Same words — two totally different places. So remember: use say when you want the penguin to show a speech bubble; use print when you want to write a message in the terminal.
gui.tutorials.pythonHello.recap: Congrats — you just wrote your first Python program! Two lines of real Python. Remember the difference: say is a CodeSkool helper — it makes your sprite speak on the stage. print is standard Python — it writes text to the terminal, and it works the same way in every Python program anywhere. Try changing the words inside the quotes on either line and click Run again to see your new message.
gui.tutorials.pythonUseAVariable.intro: A variable is like a labelled box. You pick a name (the label), and Python keeps a value inside. You can open the box by name whenever you want what's in it. You've already made these in Scratch — clicking "Make a Variable" and typing a name creates a new labelled box.
gui.tutorials.pythonUseAVariable.typeAss:
On line 1, type:

message = "Hello Python!"

This is Scratch's orange "set my variable to Hello Python!" block, written as text. The equals sign puts the value on the right INSIDE the box labelled message. From now on, the box called message holds "Hello Python!".

Box labels can use letters, numbers, and underscores. No spaces.
gui.tutorials.pythonUseAVariable.typePri:
Press Enter for line 2, then type:

print(message)

In Python, print is like Scratch's say block — but instead of showing a speech bubble on the stage, it writes the message into the terminal at the bottom. And just like dragging the orange rounded variable block into a Scratch say block, in Python you put a variable into print by typing its name. So print(message) tells Python: open the box called message, then write what's inside into the terminal.
gui.tutorials.pythonUseAVariable.noQuote: Notice there are no quotes around the word message in the print line. That tiny detail changes everything. Without quotes, Python sees message as the name of a box and prints what's inside, which is Hello Python. If we put quotes around it instead, Python would ignore the box completely and just print the word message itself, letter by letter. The rule: no quotes means look in the box, quotes means it's just plain text.
gui.tutorials.pythonUseAVariable.runBoth:
Click Run. The terminal shows: Hello Python!

Python ran line 1 (put "Hello Python!" in the box called message) and then line 2 (opened the box and printed what was inside).
gui.tutorials.pythonUseAVariable.tryYour: Change the text in quotes on line 1 to anything you like — your name, a joke, your favourite food. Click Run again. The print line never changes — only what's inside the box does.
gui.tutorials.pythonUseAVariable.done: That's your first Python variable. You picked a name, put a value into the box with the equals sign, and opened the box with print. The same idea works for your name, a score, a list of friends — anything you want Python to remember and reuse.
gui.tutorials.pythonAskTheUser.typeInput: Type this on line 1: name = input("What is your name?"). The function input is standard Python — it prints the question in the terminal and waits for the kid to type an answer. Whatever they type is stored in the name variable. The assignment works exactly the same as the variable you made in the last tutorial.
gui.tutorials.pythonAskTheUser.typePrint: Press Enter for line 2, then type print("Hello, " + name). The plus sign glues two pieces of text together into one — this is called concatenation. "Hello, " is a fixed string, name holds whatever the kid typed. Together they become one greeting.
gui.tutorials.pythonAskTheUser.runAndAns: Click Run. The terminal prints the question and waits. Click inside the terminal, type your name, and press Enter. Python glues the two strings together and prints the greeting with your name at the end.
gui.tutorials.pythonAskTheUser.recap: Now change the question inside the quotes on line 1 — ask for a favourite colour, food, or game — and change the greeting on line 2 to match. Click Run again. Every time the program asks something new, the kid's answer flows into the second line through the variable.
gui.tutorials.pythonNumbers.typeInput: Type this on line 1: age = int(input("How old are you?")). input always gives back text — even when the kid types 10, Python sees the string "10". The function int is standard Python too — it converts that text into a real number the computer can do maths with. Without int, Python would glue the number into the greeting as text and you could not add to it.
gui.tutorials.pythonNumbers.typePrint: Press Enter for line 2, then type print("You will be " + str(age + 1) + " next year"). age + 1 is normal maths — the next birthday number. str is standard Python too — it turns the number back into text so the plus sign can glue it to the rest of the sentence. You need str here because Python refuses to glue text and numbers together directly.
gui.tutorials.pythonNumbers.runAndAnswer: Click Run. The terminal asks the question. Click the terminal, type your age as a whole number, press Enter. Python reads the text, turns it into a number with int, adds one, then turns the result back into text so the whole sentence prints as one line.
gui.tutorials.pythonNumbers.recap: Run it again with a different age. Try 9, 99, even 0. Each time, int turns the text you typed into a real number, Python adds one, and str turns the answer back into text for the sentence. Maths and text, working together on the same line.
gui.tutorials.pythonTypeConversionFixThe: Type these two lines — ask for a first number, then a second. Notice we are storing them in a and b without wrapping in int this time. input always gives back text, so a and b hold strings like "12" and "34" even when the kid types digits. That's the whole point of this tutorial — see what happens when text pretends to be a number.

Now type print(a + b). This looks like it should add two numbers but it doesn't. Because a and b are strings, the plus sign glues the text together instead of adding. Type 12 for a and 34 for b, and Python will print "1234" — not the answer 46.

Add a fourth line: print(int(a) + int(b)). int is standard Python — it converts a string of digits into a real number. Now the plus sign adds numbers together properly. The same values 12 and 34 now give 46. Same variables, same plus — different result, just because of the conversion.

Click Run, type 12, type 34. The terminal prints "1234" from the wrong line and "46" from the right line. Remember: input gives you text. To do maths on input, always wrap it in int (for whole numbers) or float (for decimals) first. Without that step, plus glues instead of adds.
gui.tutorials.pythonMakeAChoice.typeInpu: Type line 1: age = int(input("How old are you?")). Same pattern as the last tutorials — ask a question, convert to a number. This tutorial adds something new: the program will react differently depending on the answer.
gui.tutorials.pythonMakeAChoice.typeIf:
Leave a blank line and then type these two lines:

if age >= 10:
    print("You can join!")

The if keyword is standard Python. It means only run the indented line below when the condition is true. The condition age >= 10 is checked — if true, Python prints the welcome. The four spaces before print are the indentation — Python uses them to know which lines belong to the if.
gui.tutorials.pythonMakeAChoice.typeElse:
Type these two lines next:

else:
    print("You are too young")

else is Python's way of saying "otherwise" — the indented line under it only runs when the if's condition was false. Together the if and else cover both possibilities: either the kid is old enough or they are not.
gui.tutorials.pythonMakeAChoice.run: Click Run. Type an age of 12 — Python prints "You can join!" because 12 is greater than or equal to 10. Run it again and type 5 — Python prints "You are too young" because 5 is less than 10. One program, two different paths, depending on the answer.
gui.tutorials.pythonBooleanLogic.typeVar:
Type these three lines:

age = 15
has_pass = True
vip = False

age holds a number. has_pass and vip hold True and False — those are Python's two special values for yes and no. They're called booleans. You'll use them to make decisions.
gui.tutorials.pythonBooleanLogic.typeAnd:
Leave a blank line, then type:

if age > 10 and has_pass:
    print("Allowed (AND)")

and means both sides must be true. age > 10 is true (15 is more than 10) and has_pass is true, so Python prints the line. If either side were false, Python would skip it.
gui.tutorials.pythonBooleanLogic.typeOr:
Type next:

if age > 18 or has_pass:
    print("Allowed (OR)")

or means at least one side must be true. age > 18 is false (15 is not more than 18) but has_pass is true — so the whole thing is true and the line prints.
gui.tutorials.pythonBooleanLogic.typeNot:
Type next:

if not has_pass:
    print("No pass")

not flips true to false and false to true. has_pass is true, so not has_pass is false — nothing prints. Change has_pass to False at the top and run again; now the line will print.
gui.tutorials.pythonGuessTheNumber.setup:
Type these three setup lines:

print("🎯 Guess the number!")

number = 5

guess = int(input("Enter your guess: "))

number is the secret answer — hardcoded to 5 for now. guess holds whatever the kid types, wrapped in int so it's a real number we can compare.
gui.tutorials.pythonGuessTheNumber.run: Click Run. Type 3 — Python says "📉 Too low". Run again, type 9 — "📈 Too high". Run once more, type 5 — "🎉 Correct!". Same program, three different endings — each one picked by elif.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.pinModeBtnInput: Drag a second pinMode block below the first. Set pin to 2 and change mode to input. input is the opposite of output — instead of sending voltage out, pin 2 will listen for voltage coming in. That's how the Arduino will know when you've pressed the button.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.pinModePullup: There's a problem with plain input: when a pin is connected to nothing, the reading flips between high and low randomly. The Arduino can't tell what's really happening on that pin. Click the mode dropdown and change it to input pullup. This tells the Arduino to quietly hold pin 2 at high (5 volts) all on its own, until something specific changes that. Now the readings are stable.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.dropEquals: Open the Operators category. Drag the equals block. Drop it into the diamond slot of the if-else — that's where the yes-or-no question goes. The equals block compares two things; we'll fill in what to compare in the next two steps.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.dropRead: Back to Arduino. Drag a digitalRead block. Drop it into the left slot of the equals block. Set its pin to 2. digitalRead is how a program senses the world — it checks what voltage is sitting on a pin right now and reports back high (5 volts) or low (0 volts). It doesn't change anything; it just looks.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.dropLow: Still in the Arduino category, drag a low block. Drop it into the right slot of the equals block. Your question now reads: 'is pin 2 low right now?' Or in plain words: 'is pin 2 at 0 volts right now?'
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.whyLow: Strange, right? Pressing should mean on, not off. Here's why. input pullup holds pin 2 at high (5 volts) all the time. The button is wired so pressing connects pin 2 to a special wire that's at 0 volts. That pulls pin 2's reading down from 5 volts to 0 volts — which is low. So pressed = low. We'll meet that 0-volt wire when we wire up the button.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.meetWrite: We've used digitalRead to listen. Now meet its twin: digitalWrite — the block that lets your program control things. digitalWrite takes two inputs: a pin number and a value (high or low). When you set a pin high, the Arduino puts 5 volts on it. When you set it low, 0 volts. That's it. Connect an LED, a buzzer, a motor driver, or even another Arduino — digitalWrite is how your program turns them on and off. We'll drop two of these next: one for when the button is pressed, one for when it isn't.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.meetGround: A battery has two terminals — positive (+) and negative (−). On an Arduino, the negative terminal is called ground, often labelled GND on the board. Ground is just a name for 0 volts. Now we can tell the full story of our button. When the button is not pressed, input pullup is gently holding pin 2 at 5 volts — so digitalRead reports high. When you press the button, it connects pin 2 directly to ground. That direct connection beats the gentle pullup, so pin 2 drops to 0 volts — and digitalRead reports low. that'S why pressed = low and not-pressed = high. The mystery from the question we built earlier is solved.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.experiments: Try this: swap high and low in the two digitalWrite blocks. Now pressing turns the LED off, and letting go turns it on — a backwards lamp. Or add a second LED on pin 12, give it its own pinMode and digitalWrites, and make it light up only when the button is released. Two LEDs, opposite reactions, same button.
gui.tutorials.buttonLed.recap: Five minutes ago you didn't know any of this. Now you do: pinMode tells the Arduino which pins send voltage and which listen for it. digitalWrite tells a pin to be high (5 volts) or low (0 volts) — that's how programs control the world. digitalRead asks a pin what voltage is on it right now — that's how programs sense the world. if-else lets your program decide between two paths — same as Scratch. ground, often labelled GND, is the name for the 0-volt wire — the reference point for everything else. Programs that read the world, decide, and react are everywhere — alarms, traffic lights, smart locks, your phone's buttons. You can write them now.
